title.en: Affidavits for Gap Year, Single Status, Income and More — Format, Stamp Paper and Notary

answerBox.en: An affidavit is a sworn written statement on stamp paper (₹10–100 in most states), signed before a notary or oath commissioner. Colleges, employers, embassies and departments demand them for gap years, single status, income declarations, address proof and lost documents. Making one takes an hour: correct format, stamp paper, notarisation — ₹199 gets it professionally drafted.

en.label: Validity
en.startsFrom: some offices want recent (3–6 months) ones

en.title: Get the recital right for the purpose
en.summary: Each affidavit type has expected contents — match them.
en.detail: Common types and their core recitals: **gap-year** (period of gap, truthful reason — self-study/health/family, and no criminal proceedings during it); **single/unmarried status** (for visas/marriage abroad — status, sometimes parents' confirmation); **income affidavit** (annual income, sources — for scholarships/fee waivers/EWS); **address proof**, **name one-and-the-same**, **lost document** declarations. The receiving institution's format wins where prescribed — check their website first.

en.title: Stamp paper + deponent details
en.summary: ₹10–100 non-judicial stamp paper; exact ID-matching names.
en.detail: Buy **non-judicial stamp paper/e-stamp** of your state's affidavit denomination (commonly **₹10–₹100**). The affidavit opens with your name, parentage, age and address **exactly as per ID**, states the facts in numbered paragraphs in first person, and ends with the verification clause ("verified that the contents are true to my knowledge"). Our **₹199 service** drafts any affidavit to the receiving office's requirements.

en.title: Swear it before the notary
en.summary: Sign in the notary's presence with ID; get the register entry.
en.detail: Sign before a **notary public or oath commissioner** with your original ID; the notary attests, stamps, and enters it in the notarial register (₹20–100 typical charge, regulated). For use **abroad**, you may instead need attestation chains — Home Department + **MEA apostille** (for Hague Convention countries) or embassy legalisation. Courts require oath-commissioner attestation for court affidavits — venue matters.

en.title: Know the weight of what you sign
en.summary: A false affidavit is perjury — not paperwork.
en.detail: Affidavits are sworn statements: deliberate falsehood is punishable — **Section 193 IPC (now Section 229 BNS)**, up to 7 years where used in judicial proceedings, and Section 199/200 IPC (now 227/228 BNS) for false declarations. Institutions also cancel admissions/appointments gained on false affidavits years later. State facts you can stand behind; where truth is nuanced (gap year with a failed venture), state it truthfully — honest recitals are accepted daily.

rows.label: Professional drafting
rows.note: WakilBhai; any affidavit type, same day

en.footnote: Institutions reject affidavits for stale dates, name mismatches with ID, and missing verification clauses more than anything else — the ₹199 draft exists to kill those rejections.

en.q: What stamp paper value does an affidavit need?
en.a: The state-notified denomination for affidavits — ₹10 in many states, ₹20–₹100 in others; e-stamps are equivalent. Higher denominations are never wrong, just wasteful. Court affidavits in some states go on plain paper with court-fee stamps — follow the court's practice.

en.q: Is notarisation compulsory, or is my signature enough?
en.a: An affidavit is by definition sworn — attestation by a notary/oath commissioner is what converts your statement into an affidavit. Unattested "affidavits" are mere declarations; some schemes accept self-declarations instead (post-2014 self-attestation push), so check whether the office wants an affidavit or a self-declaration — the ₹199 covers either.

en.q: How recent must an affidavit be?
en.a: The affidavit itself doesn't expire, but institutions commonly want one sworn within 3–6 months for status-type facts (single status, income) since those change. Date-sensitive purposes (visa files) should be sworn fresh close to submission.

en.q: Can an affidavit be made in English and Hindi both?
en.a: Either language works across most of India; embassies typically want English. Bilingual affidavits (English with vernacular translation) are accepted and sometimes required for regional offices. What matters is the deponent understanding the contents — the notary is supposed to confirm this.

en.q: What happens if facts in my affidavit later turn out wrong?
en.a: Innocent errors can be corrected by a fresh corrective affidavit explaining the mistake. Deliberate falsehood is perjury (193 IPC, now 229 BNS) and voids whatever was obtained on it — degrees, appointments, licences included, with no limitation on when institutions can act.

reviewer: Adv. Arjun Nair, Bar Council of Kerala, KER/0973/2012
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
